title: New cardiac guidelines mean more statins for more people, study finds
summary: Instead of looking at a person's risk over the next 10 years, starting at age 40, doctors are ‌now advised to consider the ⁠patient's risk ⁠over the next several decades, starting at age 30.
thbAction: Position THB's pharma CRM and omnichannel HCP engagement workflows to support launch planning, KAM orchestration, and compliant follow-up across India and the Middle East.
contentHtml:
NEW GUIDELINES WILL HAVE MORE ADULTS USING STATIN ​DRUGSRecently issued recommendations will significantly change how doctors assess and manage cardiovascular risk and will increase the number of people for whom cholesterol-lowering statin drugs are recommended, studies published on Monday in JAMA suggest.The recommendations were issued jointly in March by the American Heart Association, the American College of Cardiology, and other major organizations.

Instead of looking at a person's risk over the next 10 years, starting at age 40, doctors are ‌now advised to consider the ⁠patient's risk ⁠over the next several decades, starting at age 30.

Advt

Previously, doctors generally advised people with high cholesterol in their 30s and 40s to focus on diet and exercise.Now, people who are generally considered to be at low risk in the shorter term, meaning they are under 3% risk of having a heart attack or stroke within 10 years, but at modest risk - over 10% - within 30 years, will be advised to start taking statins.

Furthermore, treatment targets for "bad" low-density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol have been lowered to 70 mg/dL for most patients without a history of cardiovascular events and to 55 mg/dL for high-risk patients.In one new study, researchers estimated that as a result of the lower LDL targets and longer-term risk assessments, cholesterol-lowering statin drugs will likely be recommended to an additional 21.5 million Americans, bringing ​the total to 87.5 million, or 56.6% of adults ages 30 to 79.

"The shift to a longer view ⁠of cardiovascular disease ‌risk is a sea change for doctors in counseling patients," study leader Dr. Timothy S. Anderson of the University of Pittsburgh said in a statement."Observational ​evidence suggests the longer ​people are exposed to high levels of inflammatory cholesterol molecules, the greater their downstream risks of heart attacks and strokes are," Anderson said.

"Unfortunately, we ⁠do not have randomized clinical trials for low-risk people 30 years out that would directly support this new ​understanding of how risk builds more gradually over the years," he said.

Advt

Ultimately, he added, patients should talk to their doctors about ​balancing the potential for modest cardiovascular risk reduction against the potential for adverse drug events and costs.

"For patients seeking to fully minimize risks of heart attacks and strokes, early statin therapy may be a good choice," Anderson said.An accompanying study by a separate team estimated that the proportions of U.S. adults with LDL levels above the new targets are 9.9% among low-risk adults, 63.6% among borderline/intermediate-risk adults, and 82.7% among high-risk adults.E-BIKE INJURIES ON THE RISE, WITH SEVERE CONSEQUENCES

Advt

A California study found injuries involving electric bicycles, or e-bikes, have spiked dramatically there, rising from just two reported collisions in 2018 to 1,506 in 2024 - while rates of bicycle injuries remained stable.E-bike riders were more likely to experience moderate-to-severe injuries than riders of traditional ‌bicycles, the researchers also found."E-bikes provide valuable transportation and recreational benefits, but their higher speeds can generate crash forces more comparable to motorcycles than traditional bicycles," study leader Dr. John Austin of UC San Diego School of Medicine said in a statement.

E-bikes allow riders to travel farther and faster with ​less physical effort than traditional ​bicycles - but they are generally heavier and capable of ⁠higher speeds than conventional bicycles, factors that may contribute to more serious injuries when collisions occur, his team wrote in Trauma Surgery and Acute Care Open.The researchers analyzed data in the California Highway Patrol's Statewide Integrated Traffic Records System collected from 2018 through 2024 on 4,035 e-bike collisions and 58,332 traditional bicycle collisions.Along with the sharp increase in e-bike collisions, ​they also found injured e-bike riders were younger, had more moderate-to-severe injuries, and were more likely to be involved in violations related to unsafe speeds and improper turns, compared to injured riders of traditional bicycles.The researchers say the results support efforts to improve rider education, encourage consistent helmet use and expand infrastructure designed to protect cyclists.They also point to the potential value of policies that address issues such as age requirements, e-bike classification standards and safe riding practices."Parents need to understand what they are buying for their children, and communities and policymakers need to recognize that safety infrastructure, education and policy have not kept pace with adoption," study coauthor Dr. Jay Doucet of Florida State University at Lee Health said in a statement."

  • Reuters
